##  [86] "LOC100740942" "LOC100741538" "LOC100742379" "LOC117151323" "LOC112212298"
##  [91] "LOC100745971" "LOC100746922" "LOC112212342" "LOC100747482" "LOC100741733"
##  [96] "LOC100743659" "LOC112212391" "LOC117151378" "LOC112212457" "LOC117151400"
## [101] "LOC105680464" "LOC112212490" "LOC100746925" "LOC100742896" "LOC100749164"
## [106] "LOC100741338" "LOC100747761" "LOC100743548" "LOC100745212" "LOC100747724"
## [111] "LOC100747923" "LOC100749557" "LOC100741662" "LOC100741899" "LOC100749476"
## [116] "LOC100746492" "LOC100748764" "LOC100740703" "LOC100740944" "LOC100749998"
## [121] "LOC100744294" "LOC117151539" "LOC112212676" "TRNAQ-UUG_2"  "TRNAL-UAG"   
## [126] "LOC105680658" "LOC100749593" "LOC105680662" "LOC100743069" "LOC100744251"
## [131] "LOC117151579" "LOC100750118" "LOC105680687" "LOC100748487" "TRNAM-CAU_3" 
## [136] "LOC100746337" "LOC100747164" "LOC100742384" "LOC100748129" "LOC100749526"
## [141] "LOC100743018" "LOC117151617" "LOC100740019" "LOC117151627" "LOC112212810"
## [146] "LOC100748130" "LOC100742146" "LOC100741463" "LOC100741820" "LOC100745497"
## [151] "LOC100741790" "LOC105680791" "LOC100746499" "LOC100750036" "LOC117151691"
## [156] "LOC117151701" "LOC100743382" "LOC100744063" "LOC100747648" "LOC117151717"
## [161] "LOC117151727" "LOC100750006" "LOC117151743" "LOC100749295" "LOC100744421"
## [166] "LOC100745139" "LOC100745379" "LOC100744972" "LOC112212951" "LOC100745862"
## [171] "LOC100746778" "LOC100745140" "LOC100740464" "LOC112212976" "LOC112212977"
## [176] "LOC100744615" "LOC100748936" "LOC100748097" "LOC100744024" "LOC100745541"
## [181] "LOC117151790" "LOC100742985" "LOC105680924" "LOC117151810" "LOC100741979"
## [186] "LOC100747251" "LOC117151834" "LOC105680934" "LOC100743710" "LOC100745702"
## [191] "LOC100747932" "LOC117151865" "LOC112213157" "LOC100750082" "LOC100750202"
## [196] "LOC117151886" "LOC100741914" "LOC112213189" "LOC112213210" "LOC117151919"
## [201] "LOC112213234" "LOC100749363" "LOC105681075" "LOC100743988" "LOC100740105"
## [206] "LOC100741864" "LOC100740423" "TRNAS-AGA_2"  "LOC100742876"

On the above volcano plot, the y-axis is the \(-log_{10}\) \(p\)-value and the x-axis is the \(log_2\)-fold change. The dashed gold lines show where \(log_2\)-fold change between the chill coma and control conditions is \(\geq 2\), which is would mean that expression is \(2^2\) times greater in one condition over the other in linear change. Thus, the interpretation of this volcano plot (as printed using lfc <- lfc_thresholds[4], which MAY or MAY NOT be your selection from Question 23A!) is that there is a single gene, LOC100742138, significantly up-regulated in the chill coma condition relative to the control condition such that expression was more than 4 times in the chill coma condition as control. Note that I have used ggrepel() to annotate genes with a \(log_2\)-fold change of greater than the threshold.

Question 23B: [1.5 points]

Interpret your version of the volcano plot using the \(log_2\)-fold change threshold from Question 23A. Does the same gene LOC100742138 appear to be significantly differentially expressed (and up-regulated) regardless of which LFC threshold you choose? (If you’re not sure, look for it in the volcano dataframe!). Why is this?

(JML) In Q13B, I selected LFC threshold of 0.0, so the index used in the volcano plot is 1. The gene LOC100742138 does not appear to be significantly differentially expressed in LFC threshold of 1.

What happened to the dashed gold line in your plot vs. the one I made? (Refer back to the HTML document if needed). Why did this happen?

(JML) If I’m understanding this properly, when the log2-fold change is 2, the LFC threshold is also 2, so the gold lines in Figure 8 are drawn at +/-2. For our LFC threshold of 0, there would only be one line at the 0 log2-fold mark.

4 Machine Learning Analyses

Thus far, you have likely been primarily exposed to the kitchen sink model of algorithm selection. That’s pretty common in many applications of data science. In some fields, however – like biology and biomedicine – folks can sometimes be a little more hesitant to just ‘throw anything and everything’ at their data. Why?

  • It contrasts fundamentally with how biologists think about performing “science”.

  • They want clear, methodologies that they can understand (transparency is key).

  • The statistics involved were not taught to them.

  • There is a very real (and sometimes justified) fear over p-hacking and a fundamental misunderstanding that optimization (which is what we do in ML) is NOT the same thing.

  • Every good analysis requires clearly justified choices.

This leads many scientists to use more conventional methods, like DESeq2 because they are more validated - and thus more trusted. And there is NOTHING wrong with the conventional approaches – when they work! I’ve published several times using DESeq2 and I will continue to do so.

So, why would I even consider switching?

One word: NOISE.

4.1 Random Forest

You will likely recall that Random Forest (RF) is a popular supervised learning algorithm, or learner, with few assumptions about the relationships among variables. As we discussed in Project 1, RF is generally well-suited to capture complex interactions such as those commonly seen in biological systems and can run either a regression or classification.

It is based on the binary decision tree, which progressively splits the samples into two child nodes that maximize how much of the variance in the dependent variable is explained by the predictors. However, as briefly discussed in Project 1, single decision trees tend to over fit the data, i.e., they tend to generate much better predictions on the training set than on the test set. Thus, RF uses an ensemble of trees (a forest!) to counteract this overfitting (although note that it NO algorithm is robust to overfitting!). Each tree in the forest uses a random sample of the observations, while only a subset of all features (columns) is assessed for each node split.

Although sometimes called a ‘black box’ algorithm, RF is more explainable than other deep-learning methods. This tends to make researchers a bit more hesitant to use it. However, recall that RF can give you is a scoring of features according to their influence; this is a measure of how important the feature was in generating the trees in the forest. The result is a ranked list of features that were important for predicting the outcome.

RF is being used increasingly with gene expression studies because, as an ensemble learning algorithm, it is thought to be useful in what is often referred to as the “large \(P\) small \(n\)” paradigm, where \(P\) is the number of predictors and \(n\) is the number of observations. You may not fully realize it, but we have just such a \(P >> n\) problem here: \(n\) is actually the number of samples whereas \(P\) is the number of genes!

So, let’s see how a Random Forest performs out-of-bag (OOB) on our data with minimal pre-processing, filtering, and no feature selection.

4.1.1 Pre-processing the data

This time through, you are doing very minimal pre-processing. You must first extract and transpose the VST data that you got out of DESeq2, however:

## Extract the VST data
tVSdata <- t(assay(vsData))

## We do have gene names, e.g., TRNAV-UAC that are malformed for ranger and 
## randomForest. We will fix that before proceeding:
for (c in 1:ncol(tVSdata)) {
  colName <- colnames(tVSdata)[c]
  colName <- gsub("-", "_", colName)
  colName -> colnames(tVSdata)[c]
}

4.1.2 We need to put our other features (Colony, Time) and our outcome (Treatment) into the transposed matrix

Make sure to put Treatment as the last column; that will be useful downstream in the analysis pipeline!

## Create a temporary df of what we need; note that we don't need the 
## size factors, though!
df1 <- cbind(colData(dsgnObject)[1], 
             colData(dsgnObject)[3], 
             colData(dsgnObject)[2])       
## Now merge this with the transposed VST data:
tVSdata <- merge(tVSdata, df1, by = "row.names")

## The merge turns back into a dataframe and removes the sample names from the 
## rows; let's put them back:
rownames(tVSdata) <- tVSdata[,1]
## Then drop the sample names again
tVSdata <- tVSdata[,-1]

## Make sure that Treatment is a factor in the right order:
tVSdata$Treatment <- relevel(tVSdata$Treatment, ref = "Control")

4.1.3 Create the test-train partitions using the createDataPartition() function from the caret package.

This allows you to split with respect to the response variable, Treatment, to ensure that it is even distributed throughout the test-train split. You will see that, with so few samples, I am opting for a 80%-20% split. There is a reason for this; if you calculated this as you’ve done prior, e.g., using the \(\sqrt{p}:1\), you would not have sufficient training data, sadly. This is BECAUSE you have a \(P >> n\) problem!!!

library(caret)
## Make the train and test partitions:
ind <- createDataPartition(
  y = tVSdata[, c("Treatment")],     ## Treatment is evenly distributed
  p = 0.8,                           ## % into training
  list = FALSE)                      ## don't return a list

## Make training and testing
train <- tVSdata[ind, ]
test <- tVSdata[-ind,]

Figure 9. Distribution of samples between the training and testing sets.

dim(train)
## [1]    60 12927
dim(test)
## [1]    14 12927

Question 24A: [0.5 points]

How many samples (observations) are in the training and testing data sets? How many features (genes) are there? What is the ratio of predictors (genes) to samples?

(JML) There are 14 observations in the testing data set and 60 in the training data set. There are 12927 genes. The ratio of predictors to samples is 12927:74 (~175:1)

## Your optional code here.

Question 24B: [0.5 points]

Remember, we used caret’s createDataPartition() Using Figure 9, do you notice anything concerning about the training split? What about the testing split? Does adjusting the split seem to make it better, worse, or stay the same? Make sure to change it back after you are finished playing with it.

(JML) I noticed that the assignment of treatments within each time range in the testing data set is not as diverse as the assignment within the training data set. When I changed the ratio to 70:30, I saw a mild improvement. When I changed the ratio to 60:40, the diversity was even better.

Question 24C: [1 point]

What are the predictors in the training and testing sets? Can you guess what the target will be? (Hint: This will be a classification problem!). If you’re still not sure, think about what you think the roles of Treatment, Time and Colony will be in the model.

(JML) The predictors in the training & testing sets are the genes. To me, the target is identifying whether the genes are significantly differentially expressed or not, if the goal is a binary classification. I think that Treatment, Time, and Colony could be used to identify important features.

4.1.4 Fitting an OOB Random Forest model

Recall from Project 2 that an OOB random forest model is a quick, unbiased estimate of the model fitted to the training data. Each decision tree is trained on a bootstrap sample (a random sample with replacement) of the training data such that, on average, about one-third of the data is left out of that bootstrap. The data held out becomes the “out-of-bag” observations for that tree. We can use an OOB with ensemble methods, like random forest, to avoid cross-validation; notably, it can be used for a quick assessment of the suitability of a model or how prone to overfitting that model is!

Take a look at the out-of-bag (OOB) performance of a random forest model on the training data. Notice that the model is attempting to classify whether a sample was treated with cold-exposure (chill coma) or control. This may take your computer a minute (or three) to run. Be patient.

rfOOB <- randomForest::randomForest(
  Treatment ~ .,  ## Fit against ALL of the predictors (genes) + Time + Colony!
  data = train)   ## Make sure to still use the training set here!
Table 4. Results of the OOB Random Forest
Control Cold class.error
Control 14 14 0.5000
Cold 10 22 0.3125

The confusion matrix gives you the OOB classification error of the cold-exposed (chill coma) vs. the reference Control condition.

Question 24D: [1 point]

How did the model perform? Where is the classification error highest? Does this suggestion a problem with separating the Cold class from the Control class or not?

(JML) The model accurately predicted the classification approx 62% of the time, being able to predict the Control classification much less frequently than the Cold classification (50% vs ~72%, respectively). However, I’m not sure if this means that there is a problem separating Cold and Control classifications. The model seems to predict the Cold classification pretty well?

You can also plot the results of the OOB RF to assess how well the classes are separated.

Figure 10. Result of out-of-bag (OOB) random forest classifier on cold-exposed vs. control changes in gene expression.

plot(rfOOB, 
     main = "OOB Random Forest", 
     lwd = 2)

Question 24E: [1 point]

Based on Figure 10, did the OOB RF manage to find complete separation between cold-exposed and control treatments? If yes, approximately how many trees (tree depth) are needed to sufficiently distinguish the Cold and Control treatment classes? Does this suggest that using 500 trees will significantly improve the model, why or why not?

(JML) Yes, I think the OOB RF did manage to find the separation, and I think the separation began at approx 75 trees. I do not think that using 500 trees would significantly improve the model because 1) no improved differentiation was visible, 2) overfitting would become an increasing risk, and 3) there would be wasted computation time.

4.1.5 Assessing training vs. testing accuracy

Next, look at the results of the training set when tested against the data (the training error):

pred.train.rf <- predict(rfOOB, train, type = 'response')
confMat <- confusionMatrix(train$Treatment,
                pred.train.rf)
Table 5. OOB RF Train - Results of Confusion Matrix
overall
Accuracy 1.000
Kappa 1.000
AccuracyLower 0.940
AccuracyUpper 1.000
AccuracyNull 0.533
AccuracyPValue 0.000
McnemarPValue NaN

Question 24F: [0.5 points]

Look at the results of the training error. What is the accuracy and is this concerning or a good sign?

(JML) The Accuracy = 100%. I’d be very skeptical of these results because we’ve achieved perfect predictability against the training data, which makes me wonder if we will see overfitting when we look at the testing data set results.

Now, look at the results of the training set when tested against the test set (a.k.a., test error from which we derive model accuracy):

pred.test.rf <- predict(rfOOB, test, type = "response")
confMat <- confusionMatrix(pred.test.rf, test$Treatment)
print(confMat$table)
##           Reference
## Prediction Control Cold
##    Control       5    2
##    Cold          2    5
Table 6. OOB RF Test - Results of Confusion Matrix
overall
Accuracy 0.714
Kappa 0.429
AccuracyLower 0.419
AccuracyUpper 0.916
AccuracyNull 0.500
AccuracyPValue 0.090
McnemarPValue 1.000

Question 24G: [1.5 points]

Take a look at the test accuracy. It’s only 42.9%! This means it is performing WORSE than random guessing (50%). Take a closer look at Cohen’s Kappa and the McNemar’s Test. What do these tests do and what do the results tell you? Do you have any ideas why this may have occurred? (Hint: What does the high training accuracy tell us relative to the testing accuracy?)

(JML) The Kappa value of .571 indicates a moderate agreement between Control and Cold predictions beyond chance. The McNemar test value of .248 suggests that the errors could be significant. The overall accuracy of model against the testing dataset is not very good. This could suggest that the model was overfit.

4.1.6 Extracting Important Genes from an OOB Random Forest model

Even though it’s a severely underperforming model in terms of predicting outcomes from unseen data (test accuracy), we can still extract the top important features:

importantRF <- rfOOB$importance     ## Store the most important genes!
Table 7. Top 10 important genes identiftied by the OOB Random Forest
MeanDecreaseGini
LOC100744751 0.1030959
LOC100745204 0.1014580
LOC100747754 0.1009642
LOC105680701 0.0979910
LOC100740663 0.0952136
LOC100743631 0.0922944
LOC100746804 0.0869063
LOC100740150 0.0806433
LOC100744675 0.0785075
LOC100748804 0.0756574

Question 25: [3 points]

Write code that will compare the resulting important genes from the RF (Hint: take the Mean Decrease Gini that is greater than 0) and compare that to the genes stored in res that are significant DE genes per DESeq2. How many overlapping genes did you find and what % overlap is that? (Hint 2: You may find anti_join() (to find differing rows) or intersect() (to find overlapping rows) helpful here.)

Hint 3: Don’t forget to make sure to filter the DESeq2 results for signifiance using the adjusted \(p\)-value!

Hint 4: You may find it helpful to turn importantRF into a dataframe first; note that the genes are in the rownames.

(JML) I’m taking a stab at this answer, but I’m not sure I’ve done this properly. For the RF, I took importantRF with MeanDecreaseGini > 0. For the res, I used similar code to how the volcano dataframe was created. Then, I created vectors of the rownames (gene names) and then identified the intersection of the two. This resulted in 114 common genes - I think this might have an error though. There should only be 9.

#using a test lfc value; this didn't seem to make a difference though :(
lfcx = 4

# for Gini
rfOOBdf <- importantRF %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  filter(MeanDecreaseGini > 0) 

rfOOBvector <- rownames(rfOOBdf)

# for DEG
resdf <- res %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(DEG = ifelse(log2FoldChange > lfcx & padj < 0.05, "Up-regulated",
              ifelse(log2FoldChange < -lfcx & padj < 0.05, "Down-regulated", "N.S."))) %>%
  drop_na %>%
  filter(DEG != "N.S.")

resdfvector <- rownames(resdf)

# to compare
same_between_both <- intersect(resdfvector, rfOOBvector)
print(same_between_both)
## character(0)

Question 26: [2 points]

Now, modify your code from Question 25 into a function. Your function should take at least two arguments:

  1. a log-fold change cutoff for the DESeq results

  2. (Optional) you may choose to add a p-value cutoff for significance.

  3. a list of important genes from RF or another machine learning algorithm.

Show that your function works for a LFC cutoff of 1 and the same important genes from the OOB RF, importantRF.

Hint: I get 9 total overlapping genes when I do this!

(JML) I made this function based on my attempt on Q25, which doesn’t yield the correct results. If the code on Q25 does get changed, we’ll need to update this function code as well.

# (JML)
compare_gene_vectors <- function(lfc_input, pval_input = 0.05, rf_input) {
# for Gini
  rfOOBdf <- rf_input %>% 
    as.data.frame() %>% 
    filter(MeanDecreaseGini > 0) 

  rfOOBvector <- rownames(rfOOBdf)
  
  # for DEG
  resdf <- res %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    mutate(DEG = ifelse(log2FoldChange > lfc_input & padj < pval_input, "Up-regulated",
                        ifelse(log2FoldChange < -lfc_input & padj < pval_input, "Down-regulated", "N.S."))) %>%
    drop_na %>%
    filter(DEG != "N.S.")
  
  resdfvector <- rownames(resdf)
  
  # to compare
  same_between_both <- intersect(resdfvector, rfOOBvector)
  print(same_between_both)
}

# Print
  cat("Number of overlapping genes:", length(same_between_both), "\n")
## Number of overlapping genes: 0
  pct_overlap <- (length(same_between_both) / length(rfOOBvector)) * 100
  cat("Overlapping genes:\n")
## Overlapping genes:
  print(same_between_both)
## character(0)
  # Return results
  #return(list(overlapping_genes = same_between_both,
              #percent_overlap = pct_overlap))

# test me
intersecting_genes <- compare_gene_vectors(1, 0.05, importantRF)
## [1] "LOC100742047" "LOC100747881" "LOC100747885" "LOC100740746" "LOC100743629"
## [6] "LOC100741350" "LOC100747180" "LOC100746398"
print(intersecting_genes)
## [1] "LOC100742047" "LOC100747881" "LOC100747885" "LOC100740746" "LOC100743629"
## [6] "LOC100741350" "LOC100747180" "LOC100746398"

Question 27: [2 points]

Overall, how did the OOB RF model perform? Which prediction (training or testing) had the highest accuracy? How many overlapping genes were found with DESeq2? Would you recommend this model as-is for benchmarking? Would you still consider this model for feature selection, cross-validation, and tuning?

AJ-.Overall, the OOB Random Forest model performed poorly in terms of predictive generalization. The training accuracy was extremely high (100%), indicating a likely case of overfitting. The test accuracy was higher than initially estimated (around 85.7%) but the small sample size makes this number unstable and unreliable for benchmarking.Using this model as-is for benchmarking or reporting definitive conclusions about predictive performance will not be recommended. However, I would still consider Random Forest for feature selection, cross-validation, and hyperparameter tuning, because ensemble methods can be very effective if tuned properly.

4.2 Support Vector Machine (SVM)

The other machine learning algorithm we are going to apply to the gene expression data is one that has been used for quite a long time now and is becoming quite conventional in its own right: SVM, or Support Vector Machine. This algorithm is especially handy for gene expression data because it can also perform a regression or classification and it does not require (although it can handle) the boundary between discrete classes to be linear.

You will do SVM this time as a classification, just as you did with Random Forest. You will be trying t tries to find the best boundary that separates the treatment classes based on the 12,000+ gene predictors, Colony, and Time, so the same model used for RF as well. First, you will use a linear kernel, in which the SVM assumes that the data can be separated using a straight line (i.e., in 2-dimensions) or a hyperplane (i.e., in higher dimensions). It will be trying to find the maximum-margin hyperplane which separates the two classes as widely as possible. Then, when the model “sees” new data, it will classify those new points based o n which side of the line (or plane) they fall onto.

Thinking back to your PCA, you might ask yourself now - do you think your data are linearly separable or close to it? (Monotonic). If not, a linear kernel is unlikely to perform well. But, never fear! SVM can utilize non-linear kernels too!

4.2.1 Pre-process the datasets

Just like with RF, you will not pre-process the data heavily or filter it in any way just to get a sense of the default performance. Then, in Project 3 you will continue this work. Normally, data for an SVM must be scaled; but you already have scaled and normalized data! Thus, there is no need to do that pre-processing here.

You also cannot give an SVM an ordered factor, so you need to fix that before moving forward. You will turn the ordered factors (Treatment, Colony, and Time) into unordered factors and then re-call the train-test split on those corrected data.

## Starting with the same data as for RF, however, unordering the ordered
## factors now
tVSdata <- tVSdata %>% 
  mutate_if(is.ordered, factor, ordered = FALSE)

## Use the same split indices and repartition the data
train <- tVSdata[ind, ]
test <- tVSdata[-ind, ]

4.2.2 Run the non-CV linear kernel SVM

As with RF, be patient and let it run, if needed. Mine only takes ~ 20 seconds, though!

## Using the e1071 package (but then variable importance is
## only going to be weights)
svm <- svm(Treatment ~ ., 
    data = train,
    kernel = "linear",
)
## [1] "The total number of support vectors was: 53"

Question 28A: [1 point]

What does the number of support vectors tell us in SVM, especially with a linear kernel? You may need to do a little research!

AJ-In an SVM, support vectors are the observations closest to the decision boundary (points used by the algorythm).A very high number of support vectors in a linear SVM strongly suggests that the classes are not linearly separable, and that the model is likely to struggle to generalize well to unseen data.56 out of 60 training samples indicates that the classes are not cleanly separable by a linear hyperplane, there is overlapping or complexly structured.

4.1.3 Find the training error

Once again, check the training accuracy to get a sense for training error.

pred.train.svm <- predict(svm, train, type = 'response')
confMat <- confusionMatrix(train$Treatment,
                pred.train.svm)
print(confMat$table)
##           Reference
## Prediction Control Cold
##    Control      28    0
##    Cold          0   32
Table 8. Linear SVM Train - Results of Confusion Matrix
overall
Accuracy 1.000
Kappa 1.000
AccuracyLower 0.940
AccuracyUpper 1.000
AccuracyNull 0.533
AccuracyPValue 0.000
McnemarPValue NaN

4.1.34 Find the test accuracy

Again, assess the testing performance.

pred.test.svm <- predict(svm, test, type = "response")
confMat <- confusionMatrix(pred.test.svm, test$Treatment)
print(confMat$table)
##           Reference
## Prediction Control Cold
##    Control       5    2
##    Cold          2    5
Table 9. Linear SVM Test - Results of Confusion Matrix
overall
Accuracy 0.714
Kappa 0.429
AccuracyLower 0.419
AccuracyUpper 0.916
AccuracyNull 0.500
AccuracyPValue 0.090
McnemarPValue 1.000

Question 28A: [1 point]

How does the training and testing accuracy compare? What does this seem to imply about the model?

AJ-The training accuracy for linear SVM model is 100% and testing accuracy is 78.6%. This discrepancy suggests that the model fits the training data very well but loses some predictive power on new or unseen data, a sign of potential overfitting, though not as extreme as the Random Forest model. Overall, the model generalizes well but will benefit from further tuning or other methods to improve test performance.

4.1.5 Extract the top important features

Unlike RF, the svm() function in e1071 does not estimate the importance of the predictors for you. We could extract the weights for the importance, as follows, for the linear hyperplane but the problem is that we would be somewhat arbitrarily cutting off where “importance” begins and ends.

## Extract weights (w) for the linear hyperplane
w <- t(svm$coefs) %*% svm$SV

## Get feature importance for ALL genes...
svmImportance <- abs(w) %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  ## Turn the dataframe from wide to long format
  pivot_longer(1:12931, names_to = "Gene", values_to = "Weight") %>% 
  arrange(desc(Weight))
Table 10. Linear SVM Top 10 Most Imporant Genes (Weighting Method)
Gene Weight
LOC117152593 0.0239590
LOC100747885 0.0218263
LOC100742347 0.0213464
LOC100743659 0.0197419
LOC100747506 0.0168257
LOC100747687 0.0163163
LOC100749578 0.0161197
TRNAS_AGA 0.0161065
LOC100749486 0.0146730
LOC100742541 0.0136720

Instead, you could leverage the caret package instead which has a built-in algorithm for deciding importance through the function varImp().

## The caret way:
svm2 <- train(Treatment ~ ., 
             data = train, 
             method = "svmLinear")

## Store the important genes into a dataframe.
svmImportance2 <- varImp(svm2)

Question 28B: [0.5 points]

How many important genes were identified using varImp()?

AJ-

svm2 <- train(Treatment ~ ., 
              data = train, 
              method = "svmLinear")
svmImportance2 <- varImp(svm2)
# Convert to a dataframe
svmImpDF <- svmImportance2$importance

# Count predictors with nonzero importance
nrow(svmImpDF[svmImpDF$Overall > 0, , drop = FALSE])
## [1] 0
## Extract the importance scores
importance_df <- svmImportance2$importance

## Count how many genes have importance > 0
sum(importance_df[,1] > 0)
## [1] 12073

Question 28C: [0.5 points]

Use your function from Question 26 - do you see any overlap between the DESeq2 genes with significant \(log_2\)-fold expression using the LFC cutoff you chose back in Question 13B? If so, what percent overlap?

Your answer here.

## Your code here.

Question 29: [5 points]

Try quickly repeating the SVM analyses with both a radial and a sigmoid kernel. You may use either the e1071 or the caret methods, but you may find e1071 easier. Hint: For e1071, you will change kernel = "radial" and kernel = "sigmoid". For caret, you will make method = "svmRadial" but for the sigmoid it gets complicated. Make sure to do both your training and testing predictions to check accuracy. Which kernel yields the lowest training error and the highest test accuracy? What do you conclude from all of this? (NOTE: I am not asking you to extract the important genes at this time.)

Your answer here.

## Your code here.

Question 30: [3 points]

At this point, which model - OOB RF or SVM (with kernel identified) - performs better on the gene expression data and why? What recommendations would you give for next steps including, but not limited to, feature selection, cross-validation, or hyperparameter tuning?

Your answer here.

5 Final Comments

At this stage, it should feel clear that we need to do SOMETHING else. If not, stop and reflect on that statement. We know there is a lot of noise in the data, and we have worked hard to preserve all of the observations and genes thus far. However, in noisy situations, feature selection and tuning can both have DRASTIC effects on the performance of our models. Which model will your team choose to tune for Project 3?

N.B.: Did you find this module fun or interesting? Would you like to work with genes and gene expression data more deeply? Interested in a research opportunity? Talk to Dr. Geist about opportunities to work on and potentially publish benchmarking studies like this!

References

Love, Michael I., Wolfgang Huber, and Simon Anders. 2014. “Moderated Estimation of Fold Change and Dispersion for RNA-Seq Data with DESeq2.” Genome Biology 15 (12): 550. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13059-014-0550-8.
R Core Team. 2019. R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing. Vienna, Austria: R Foundation for Statistical Computing. https://www.R-project.org.
Vabalas, Emma AND Poliakoff, Andrius AND Gowen. 2019. “Machine Learning Algorithm Validation with a Limited Sample Size.” PLOS ONE 14 (11): 1–20. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0224365.
Verble, Kelton M., Ellen C. Keaveny, Sarthok Rasique Rahman, Matthew J. Jenny, Michael E. Dillon, and Jeffrey D. Lozier. 2024. “A Rapid Return to Normal: Temporal Gene Expression Patterns Following Cold Exposure in the Bumble Bee Bombus Impatiens.” Journal of Experimental Biology 227 (9): jeb247040. https://doi.org/10.1242/jeb.247040.
